questions.question: Explain the term 'Software Crisis' and state three problems that led to its recognition.
keyPoints: Software crisis refers to difficulties in developing complex software within time, budget, and quality constraints
Problems: cost overruns, schedule delays, quality issues, maintainability problems, poor documentation
Coined in late 1960s at NATO conference

questions.question: List and briefly explain four phases of Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).
keyPoints: Requirements Analysis: gathering and documenting what customer needs
Design: creating system architecture and design documents
Implementation: actual coding and programming
Testing: verifying system works correctly
Deployment: releasing software to users
Maintenance: ongoing support and updates

questions.question: Differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional requirements with examples.
keyPoints: Functional requirements: what the system should do - e.g., user login, process transactions, generate reports
Non-functional requirements: quality constraints - e.g., performance (<2 sec response), security (encryption), reliability (99.9% uptime)
Functional describes features; non-functional describes quality attributes

questions.question: What is a Software Process Model? Explain the Waterfall Model with its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Software Process Model: framework defining sequence of activities for software development
Waterfall: linear sequential model with phases flowing downward
Advantages: simple, easy to understand, clear milestones, well-documented
Disadvantages: inflexible, late testing, high risk, poor for complex/changing requirements

questions.question: Explain the Spiral Model with a neat diagram showing its quadrants.
keyPoints: Spiral model combines iterative development with systematic risk analysis
Four quadrants: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
Iterative: cycles continue until complete product delivered
Advantages: handles risk, allows user feedback, flexible for changes
Disadvantages: complex, requires expertise in risk analysis

questions.question: What is Verification and Validation? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Verification: checking if software conforms to specifications - 'building the product right'
Validation: checking if software meets user needs - 'building the right product'
Verification example: reviewing design documents against requirements
Validation example: testing the software with actual users

questions.question: Define Feasibility Study and list four types of feasibility considered during project evaluation.
keyPoints: Feasibility Study: initial analysis to determine if project is worth pursuing
Technical Feasibility: available technology can implement the system
Economic Feasibility: project costs are justified by benefits
Operational Feasibility: system will be used effectively by users
Schedule Feasibility: project can be completed in required timeframe

questions.question: Explain the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) in detail. Discuss all phases with their inputs, outputs, and activities.
keyPoints: SDLC is a systematic approach to develop software with defined phases
Requirements Analysis: gather requirements, create SRS document, inputs - user expectations, outputs - SRS
System Design: create architecture, inputs - SRS, outputs - design documents
Implementation: code generation, inputs - design, outputs - source code
Testing: verify functionality, inputs - code, outputs - test reports
Deployment: install software, inputs - test reports, outputs - working software
Maintenance: fix and enhance, inputs - user feedback, outputs - updated version
Each phase has specific deliverables and review processes

questions.question: Compare and contrast Waterfall, Spiral, and Agile Software Process Models. Also explain when each model is suitable.
keyPoints: Waterfall: linear, sequential, rigid, good for well-defined requirements, disadvantages - inflexible to changes
Spiral: iterative with risk analysis, good for large complex projects, advantages - risk handling, disadvantages - complex
Agile: iterative, flexible, customer collaboration, advantages - responds to change, disadvantages - less predictable
Waterfall suitable for: small projects with clear requirements like embedded systems
Spiral suitable for: large, complex projects with uncertain requirements like ERP systems
Agile suitable for: projects with changing requirements, startups, web applications

questions.question: What is Requirements Engineering? Explain all its sub-processes in detail.
keyPoints: Requirements Engineering: systematic approach to identify, document, validate, and manage requirements
Elicitation: gathering requirements from stakeholders through interviews, surveys, observations
Analysis: studying requirements feasibility, detecting conflicts, prioritizing
Specification: documenting requirements clearly in SRS format
Validation: ensuring requirements are complete, consistent, and testable
Management: managing changes to requirements throughout project
Requirements must be unambiguous, complete, testable, and traceable

questions.question: Draw and explain the Spiral Model of software development. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and suitable project types.
keyPoints: Spiral model has four phases in each iteration: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
First cycle produces prototype, subsequent cycles refine toward final product
Each spiral represents an iteration with increasing completeness
Advantages: handles risk, allows user feedback, flexible, early defect detection
Disadvantages: complex, requires risk analysis expertise, may fail if risks not identified
Suitable for: large complex projects, high-risk projects, projects with unclear requirements
Not suitable for: small simple projects, well-understood requirements
